001 — EDA of the labelled SNI feature matrix

Author

Dan MacLean

Published

April 23, 2026

Goal

Characterise the labelled feature matrix produced by R/build_feature_matrix.R before any supervised learning: sample dimensions, class balance, per-feature missingness, univariate class-separation, within-class distribution shapes, and inter-feature redundancy. Two matrices are examined in parallel: the 11-feature canonical SNI set (as defined in ALL_PARAMS of SolNRCH_foldome/R/NRC_heatmap_analysis.R) and the full 54-feature set of numeric columns written by the authors’ resistosome_pipeline.

Hypotheses

  1. Per-feature distributions differ between helper and sensor classes.
  2. Missingness is class-correlated: α1-helix-dependent metrics (THETA_APEX_mean, L_APEX_mean, MU_H_mean) are NA for sensor proteins by construction, because sensors lack a detectable MADA motif.
  3. The 54-feature full matrix contains inter-feature redundancy from (raw, mean, sd, LCB/UCB) variants of the same underlying structural metric.

Rationale

Before fitting any classifier, visibility is needed on (a) what the learner will be asked to distinguish, (b) which features carry class information under the simplest possible ranker (univariate AUC), so the relative contribution of the full multivariate pipeline can later be judged, (c) how much of the feature matrix is inter-correlated, both to inform regularisation choices in mlr3 and to support the formal redundancy analysis planned for a later notebook, (d) whether missing-data patterns alone encode class identity — directly relevant to the design choice taken in the build step (option c: compare with and without α1-dependent features).

Setup

Code 
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(here)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(fig.path = "../results/figures/001_")

mat_11   <- readRDS(here("data", "feature_matrix_labelled_11.rds"))
mat_full <- readRDS(here("data", "feature_matrix_labelled_full.rds"))

CANONICAL_11 <- setdiff(names(mat_11), c("ID", "class"))
FULL_COLS    <- setdiff(names(mat_full), c("ID", "class"))

1. Matrix inventory

Code 
dim_tbl <- data.frame(
  matrix  = c("11-feature (canonical)", "full (all numeric)"),
  rows    = c(nrow(mat_11), nrow(mat_full)),
  cols    = c(length(CANONICAL_11), length(FULL_COLS))
)
knitr::kable(dim_tbl)
matrix rows cols
11-feature (canonical) 18 11
full (all numeric) 18 54
Code 
table(class = mat_11$class)
class
sensor helper 
     9      9
Code 
head(mat_11[, c("ID", "class")], 20)

Very low number of each class, as expected. Right number from my understanding.

2. Missingness patterns

2.1 Per-feature NA counts by class (canonical 11)

Checking pattern of missingness. Some features just can’t exist in some classes, may need to be accounted for. Could be a lazy way to classify.

Code 
na_by_class <- function(df, cols) {
  do.call(rbind, lapply(cols, function(col) {
    data.frame(
      feature  = col,
      n_helper_NA = sum(is.na(df[df$class == "helper", col])),
      n_sensor_NA = sum(is.na(df[df$class == "sensor", col])),
      n_helper    = sum(df$class == "helper"),
      n_sensor    = sum(df$class == "sensor"),
      row.names = NULL
    )
  }))
}

na_tbl_11 <- na_by_class(mat_11, CANONICAL_11)
na_tbl_11 <- na_tbl_11[order(-(na_tbl_11$n_helper_NA + na_tbl_11$n_sensor_NA)), ]
knitr::kable(na_tbl_11, row.names = FALSE)
feature n_helper_NA n_sensor_NA n_helper n_sensor
THETA_APEX_mean 1 9 9 9
L_APEX_mean 1 9 9 9
MU_H_mean 1 9 9 9
ipTM_LCB 0 0 9 9
Sum_CONTACTS 0 0 9 9
BSA_INT_PROTO 0 0 9 9
SD_THETA_ROT 0 0 9 9
S_PROTO 0 0 9 9
D_APEX 0 0 9 9
H_ABS_mean 0 0 9 9
D_MHD_P_mean 0 0 9 9

Clear missingness in sensors of some features. Note this is rotated relative to how Luedke present it, as theirs is done contrary to the conventions.

2.2 Missingness grid (canonical 11)

Code 
na_long <- pivot_longer(
  cbind(mat_11[, c("ID", "class")], is.na(mat_11[, CANONICAL_11])),
  cols = all_of(CANONICAL_11),
  names_to = "feature", values_to = "is_na"
)
na_long$feature <- factor(na_long$feature, levels = CANONICAL_11)
na_long$ID      <- factor(na_long$ID,
                          levels = mat_11$ID[order(mat_11$class, mat_11$ID)])

ggplot(na_long, aes(x = feature, y = ID, fill = is_na)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "grey80") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(`FALSE` = "white", `TRUE` = "black")) +
  facet_grid(class ~ ., scales = "free_y", space = "free_y") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "NA")

na_tbl_full <- na_tbl_full[order(-(na_tbl_full$n_helper_NA + na_tbl_full$n_sensor_NA)), ]
knitr::kable(
  na_tbl_full[na_tbl_full$n_helper_NA + na_tbl_full$n_sensor_NA > 0, ],
  row.names = FALSE
)
feature n_helper_NA n_sensor_NA n_helper n_sensor
SD_THETA_APEX 1 9 9 9
THETA_APEX_mean 1 9 9 9
THETA_APEX_sd 1 9 9 9
L_APEX_mean 1 9 9 9
L_APEX_sd 1 9 9 9
MU_H_mean 1 9 9 9
MU_H_sd 1 9 9 9
ALPHA1_LINEARITY_mean 1 9 9 9
ALPHA1_LINEARITY_sd 1 9 9 9
ALPHA1_PLDDT_mean 1 9 9 9
ALPHA1_PLDDT_sd 1 9 9 9
SD_THETA_APEX_mean 1 9 9 9
SD_THETA_APEX_sd 1 9 9 9

3. Univariate class-separation (rank AUC)

Idea here is that we have two groups and just see whether each feature is any good on its own at separating the two groups. We use Mann-Whitney’s U as a measure of how far apart the two groups sit on a common ranking of the values. Biologists usually meet AUC as the area under an ROC curve swept out by varying a threshold; the ranks-based form U / (n_pos * n_neg) used here is the same number, just computed differently. So 1 says the feature says helper, 0 says the feature says sensor, 0.5 says meh. We convert the AUC to be in [0.5, 1] so bigger means better ignoring sign.

The AUC of each feature used as a 1-D ranker is computed as the Mann–Whitney U statistic divided by n_helper * n_sensor, with NAs dropped. auc is directional (positive class = helper); auc_abs = max(auc, 1-auc) measures raw separation regardless of direction.

Code 
univariate_auc <- function(x, y, positive = "helper") {
  mask  <- !is.na(x)
  x     <- x[mask]; y <- y[mask]
  n_pos <- sum(y == positive); n_neg <- sum(y != positive)
  if (n_pos < 1 || n_neg < 1) {
    return(data.frame(auc = NA_real_, auc_abs = NA_real_,
                      n_used = length(x), n_pos = n_pos, n_neg = n_neg,
                      wilcox_p = NA_real_))
  }
  r <- rank(c(x[y == positive], x[y != positive]))
  U <- sum(r[seq_len(n_pos)]) - n_pos * (n_pos + 1) / 2
  auc <- U / (n_pos * n_neg)
  p   <- tryCatch(
    suppressWarnings(wilcox.test(x ~ y)$p.value),
    error = function(e) NA_real_
  )
  data.frame(auc = auc, auc_abs = max(auc, 1 - auc),
             n_used = length(x), n_pos = n_pos, n_neg = n_neg,
             wilcox_p = p)
}

auc_table <- function(df, cols) {
  do.call(rbind, lapply(cols, function(col) {
    res <- univariate_auc(df[[col]], df$class)
    cbind(feature = col, res)
  }))
}

3.1 Canonical 11

Code 
auc_11 <- auc_table(mat_11, CANONICAL_11)
auc_11 <- auc_11[order(-auc_11$auc_abs), ]
knitr::kable(auc_11, row.names = FALSE, digits = 3)
feature auc auc_abs n_used n_pos n_neg wilcox_p
ipTM_LCB 1.000 1.000 18 9 9 0.000
Sum_CONTACTS 1.000 1.000 18 9 9 0.000
BSA_INT_PROTO 1.000 1.000 18 9 9 0.000
SD_THETA_ROT 0.000 1.000 18 9 9 0.000
S_PROTO 0.000 1.000 18 9 9 0.000
D_APEX 0.000 1.000 18 9 9 0.000
D_MHD_P_mean 0.802 0.802 18 9 9 0.031
H_ABS_mean 0.247 0.753 18 9 9 0.077
THETA_APEX_mean NA NA 8 8 0 NA
L_APEX_mean NA NA 8 8 0 NA
MU_H_mean NA NA 8 8 0 NA

The non-NA canonical features look to separate the two classes fairly cleanly on their own. Some take higher values in helpers (ipTM_LCB, Sum_CONTACTS, BSA_INT_PROTO, D_MHD_P_mean) and some take higher values in sensors (SD_THETA_ROT, S_PROTO, D_APEX, H_ABS_mean) The three α1-helix features (THETA_APEX_mean, L_APEX_mean, MU_H_mean) are NA in every sensor so a 1-D AUC can’t be computed for them here.

auc_full <- auc_table(mat_full, FULL_COLS)
auc_full <- auc_full[order(-auc_full$auc_abs), ]

Lots of strong classifiers.

4. Feature distributions by class

4.1 Canonical 11

Code 
long_11 <- pivot_longer(
  mat_11, cols = all_of(CANONICAL_11),
  names_to = "feature", values_to = "value"
)
long_11$feature <- factor(long_11$feature, levels = CANONICAL_11)

ggplot(long_11, aes(x = class, y = value, colour = class)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0, alpha = 0.8, size = 1.5, na.rm = TRUE) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, fill = NA, width = 0.4, na.rm = TRUE) +
  facet_wrap(~ feature, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(sensor = "#e76f51", helper = "#264653")) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL)

Lots of separation between these values in sensors and helpers.

5. Inter-feature redundancy

Spearman correlation is used throughout; it is rank-based, robust to outliers, and appropriate for the small sample size (n = 18).

5.1 Canonical 11

Code 
cor_11 <- suppressWarnings(
  cor(mat_11[, CANONICAL_11], use = "pairwise.complete.obs", method = "spearman")
)

cor_long_11 <- as.data.frame(as.table(cor_11))
names(cor_long_11) <- c("x", "y", "rho")

ggplot(cor_long_11, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = rho)) +
  geom_tile() +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", rho)), size = 2.8) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#1C39BB", mid = "white", high = "#ED2939",
                       midpoint = 0, limits = c(-1, 1)) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = expression(rho))

High-correlation pairs (|ρ| ≥ 0.9), upper triangle only:

Code 
upper_pairs <- function(M, threshold = 0.9) {
  idx <- which(abs(M) >= threshold & upper.tri(M), arr.ind = TRUE)
  if (nrow(idx) == 0) return(data.frame(a = character(), b = character(), rho = numeric()))
  data.frame(
    a   = rownames(M)[idx[, 1]],
    b   = colnames(M)[idx[, 2]],
    rho = round(M[idx], 3),
    row.names = NULL
  )[order(-abs(M[idx])), ]
}
knitr::kable(upper_pairs(cor_11, 0.9), row.names = FALSE)
a b rho
SD_THETA_ROT D_APEX 0.922
Sum_CONTACTS BSA_INT_PROTO 0.905

The two pairs with |ρ| ≥ 0.9 look to carry largely overlapping information, so keeping both members of each pair probably adds little. Using the source attributions: Sum_CONTACTS (Scientist) pairs with BSA_INT_PROTO (Both), and SD_THETA_ROT (Both) pairs with D_APEX (Both). Neither redundant pair sits wholly within the Co-Scientist set.

5.2 Full matrix

Code 
cor_full <- suppressWarnings(
  cor(mat_full[, FULL_COLS], use = "pairwise.complete.obs", method = "spearman")
)

cor_long_full <- as.data.frame(as.table(cor_full))
names(cor_long_full) <- c("x", "y", "rho")

ggplot(cor_long_full, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = rho)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#1C39BB", mid = "white", high = "#ED2939",
                       midpoint = 0, limits = c(-1, 1), na.value = "grey90") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 8) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = expression(rho))

High-correlation pairs in the full matrix (|ρ| ≥ 0.95), upper triangle only:

Code 
pairs_full <- upper_pairs(cor_full, 0.95)
cat("Number of pairs with |rho| >= 0.95:", nrow(pairs_full), "\n")
Number of pairs with |rho| >= 0.95: 23
Code

Code 
hi_mask <- abs(cor_full) >= 0.95
diag(hi_mask) <- FALSE
hi_mask[is.na(hi_mask)] <- FALSE
n_partners <- rowSums(hi_mask)
sum(n_partners > 0)
[1] 24

Similar story, over 20 of the 50+ metrics are closely correlated with at least one other measure, so could usefully junk at least one of the pair.

6. Conclusion

We may need to whittle down the data we use based on this. But it looks like for the 11 we have discriminatory features we should be able to get information from.let
